name: SQL
scope: A person with INTERMEDIATE proficiency level in SQL as a competency should be able to consistently write correct, efficient queries using joins, subqueries, aggregate functions, and window functions. They apply fundamental database concepts such as normalization, indexing, and transaction management while modifying moderate-level schemas. They handle bulk data operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE) with an understanding of locking and concurrency. They interpret data patterns in BFSI-related transactional logs through exploratory data analysis. They perform performance tuning using profiling tools or EXPLAIN plans to identify bottlenecks and propose schema or index adjustments. They develop and maintain stored procedures and functions that encapsulate moderately complex business logic. They integrate SQL queries into ETL workflows or pipelines using scripting languages like Python or shell. They understand secure interactions with cloud or on-premise databases such as AWS RDS or Azure SQL Database. They identify and correct common inefficiencies in query structures and data types. They maintain awareness of role-based permissions, database security practices, and compliance needs. They communicate SQL logic and findings effectively to both technical and non-technical stakeholders. They collaborate well within project teams and guide junior peers on foundational SQL concepts.
long_scope:
Below is a suggested scope for an INTERMEDIATE level of SQL proficiency, focusing on the tasks, outcomes, and indicative tools or approaches an individual at this level should be capable of:

1. Query Construction & Fundamentals  
   • Consistently writes correct, efficient queries using standard SQL constructs (joins, subqueries, aggregate functions, window functions).  
   • Confidently applies foundational database concepts (data types, normalization, indexing, transactions) to design or modify schemas for moderate complexity.  

2. Data Manipulation & Analysis  
   • Executes bulk data manipulations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE) in batch operations with a clear understanding of locking and concurrency considerations.  
   • Performs exploratory data analysis on datasets of moderate size (e.g., BFSI-related transactional logs) and interprets key patterns or anomalies.  

3. Performance Tuning & Optimization  
   • Diagnoses and resolves common performance bottlenecks by refining queries (index utilization, query plan analysis) and making minor schema adjustments.  
   • Uses profiling tools or database EXPLAIN plans to identify inefficiencies in query execution paths and suggest straightforward optimizations.  

4. Stored Procedures & Scripting  
   • Develops and maintains stored procedures and functions for moderately complex business logic, ensuring readability and maintainability.  
   • Leverages scripting skills (e.g., basic Python or shell) to integrate SQL queries into data pipelines, schedulers, or automated workflows.  

5. Data Pipeline Integration & Collaboration  
   • Integrates SQL components into data pipelines or ETL processes, collaborating with other teams (data scientists, BI developers) to ensure smooth data flows.  
   • Understands how to interact securely and efficiently with cloud or on-premise databases (e.g., AWS RDS, Azure SQL Database, or similar services).  

6. Troubleshooting & Best Practices  
   • Identifies and corrects common inefficiencies such as suboptimal indexing, poorly structured queries, or inadequate data types.  
   • Demonstrates awareness of database security practices, role-based permissions, and basic compliance considerations relevant in the BFSI domain.  

7. Communication & Teamwork  
   • Explains SQL logic and data findings to technical and non-technical stakeholders (e.g., discussing how a query result impacts loan approval processes).  
   • Collaborates well within project teams, guiding more junior peers on foundational SQL while continuing to learn from senior-level data engineers.  

Overall, an INTERMEDIATE SQL practitioner can handle a range of everyday data engineering tasks—developing moderately complex queries, integrating SQL within data pipelines, and making performance or schema improvements—while maintaining a solid and reliable command of core database concepts.
